original_abstract: Epoxides are essential intermediates in the synthesis of pharmaceuticals and epoxy resins, yet achieving their efficient production through sustainable methods remains a challenge. In this study, we report a highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) epoxidation system utilizing a Br⁻/BrO⁻ redox mediator on an α-Fe₂O₃ (hematite) photoanode. This system achieves exceptional performance for a diverse range of alkenes, reaching selectivities greater than 99% and faradaic efficiencies up to 82 ± 4%—metrics that significantly outperform previously reported electrochemical and photoelectrochemical benchmarks. Using water as the sole oxygen source, we demonstrate that the reaction proceeds via a non-radical oxygen atom transfer (OAT) pathway. Specifically, Br⁻ is oxidized to BrO⁻ through an OAT process at the hematite surface, and the resulting BrO⁻ species subsequently transfers its oxygen atom to the alkene substrate. The combination of favorable thermodynamics and the non-radical nature of this mediated mechanism ensures high catalytic efficiency and selectivity. This Br⁻/BrO⁻-mediated strategy offers a robust and sustainable approach for the simultaneous production of high-value epoxides and green hydrogen.
humanized_abstract: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) epoxidation is a highly sought-after sustainable method for catalyst-assisted synthesis of pharmaceuticals, and epoxy resins. Here, we report on a PEC setup which uses a Br⁻/BrO⁻ redox mediator on an α-Fe₂O₃ (hematite) photoanode. The PEC setup enables the catalytic selective epoxidation of a range of alkenes at selectivities above 99% and at faradaic efficiencies of up to 82 ± 4%, which are the highest reported values so far and clearly outperform all current state-of-the-art electrochemical and photoelectrochemical epoxidation works. Water as sole source of oxygen proves to be evidence of a non-radical oxygen atom transfer (OAT) mechanism occurring at the hematite surface. Br⁻ is oxidized by OAT to BrO⁻ and the BrO⁻ then acts as a catalyst in delivering an overall non-quantum-mechanical atom transfer mechanism, which leads to an efficient, feasible and extremely selective synthesis of the targeted epoxides, as well as green hydrogen production.

Water as sole source of oxygen proves to be evidence of a non-radical oxygen atom transfer (OAT) mechanism occurring at the hematite surface. Br⁻ is oxidized by OAT to BrO⁻ and the BrO⁻ then acts as a catalyst in delivering an overall non-quantum-mechanical atom transfer mechanism, which leads to an efficient, feasible and extremely selective synthesis of the targeted epoxides, as well as green hydrogen production.
document.input: Epoxides are essential intermediates in the synthesis of pharmaceuticals and epoxy resins, yet achieving their efficient production through sustainable methods remains a challenge.
